summary: Policy-backed control for location scripting access. Microsoft documents the same device policy path and value name that the app writes, and the policy description matches the disabled behavior.

setting.name: Location Scripting
setting.casual_explanation: Some scripts and apps can access Windows location features through scripting interfaces. This policy decides whether that scripting access is allowed.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it when they want to reduce location access paths on the device, especially on managed or privacy-focused systems.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning this off reduces one path to location data, but location-aware scripts and automation can stop working.
setting.professional_notes: Sensors.admx defines both user and machine variants of DisableLocationScripting. The current app writes the machine policy at HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors\DisableLocationScripting with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the machine policy for location scripting.

allowed_values.label: Location scripting allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and scripted access to location remains allowed.

allowed_values.label: Location scripting disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Scripted access to location is blocked by policy.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisableLocationScripting = 1 under the machine policy key, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 7 and newer systems with Location and Sensors policy support

states.rationale: The machine policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: Sensors.admx contains both user and machine mappings. This record validates the machine-side mapping used by the current app.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Devices that use location-aware automation or scripts
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep ordinary location scripting behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to reduce access paths to location data.
tradeoffs: Location-aware scripts and automation can continue to work.
No explicit restriction is enforced for scripted location access.

recommended_profiles.label: Privacy focused
intended_for: Managed devices that do not need scripted location access
avoid_for: Automation scenarios that rely on location data
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to block scripts and similar automation from using location features.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You rely on scripts or apps that use scripted access to location data.
tradeoffs: Scripted access to location is blocked.
Some automation or apps that expect location scripting can stop working.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: DisableLocationScripting
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the policy that controls whether location scripting is allowed.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft Sensors.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows DisableLocationScripting for both user and machine scope with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 under the LocationAndSensors policy key.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisableLocationScripting = 1 under the machine policy key for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: Microsoft documents DisableLocationScripting directly in the ADMX_Sensors Policy CSP and maps it to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors\DisableLocationScripting. The same page describes that enabling the policy disables location scripting, and the app writes that exact machine policy surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: If you enable this policy setting, location scripting will be disabled for all users. ADMX mapping: Name DisableLocationScripting ... Registry Key Name Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors ... Registry Value Name DisableLocationScripting.
validation_proof.notes: The ADMX_Sensors Policy CSP page explicitly names the exact machine policy path and value name the app writes, and its policy description matches the disabled behavior.
